title: Death by chocolate cake
subtitle: Dense, fudgy chocolate cake with a dark ganache and chocolate buttercream.
excerpt: Death by chocolate cake is a very rich chocolate layer cake: a fudgy sponge made with melted dark chocolate, filled and frosted with chocolate buttercream, and finished with a dark ganache poured over the top. It is a showpiece cake. The sponge is denser than a standard Victoria sponge because of the melted chocolate, and it keeps well for several days.
sourceNotes: Triple-application chocolate cakes (chocolate in the sponge, filling, and coating) have been a staple of American celebration baking since the 1970s and 1980s. The Hershey's 'Perfectly Chocolate Chocolate Cake' and various 'death by chocolate' recipes circulated in American food magazines from the 1980s onwards, using either cocoa powder or melted chocolate in the batter. This recipe uses melted dark chocolate throughout for a more European flavour profile, following the pattern of French gateau au chocolat and the tradition of using ganache as both filling and coating.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the sponge layers unfrosted, wrapped separately. Defrost at room temperature before assembling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The assembled cake keeps in the fridge for three days. Remove 30 minutes before serving so the ganache softens.

recipeTools.notes: Two 20 cm round tins, greased and lined.
Heatproof bowl for melting chocolate over simmering water.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of hot cream and chocolate stirred until smooth. At room temperature it is pourable and glossy. As it cools it thickens to a spreadable consistency; in the fridge it firms further.

content.text: This cake has chocolate at every level: in the sponge, in the buttercream filling, and in the
poured over the top. The sponge is denser than a standard butter cake because of the melted dark chocolate. It holds up well under the frosting and slices cleanly.
Make the sponge layers first and cool them completely. Make the buttercream next. Make the ganache last, just before you assemble, so it is still pourable.
Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line two 20 cm round tins.
Melt 200g of {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl over barely simmering water, stirring occasionally. Set aside to cool for 10 minutes.
Beat {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{light-brown-sugar}} until pale and fluffy, about 3 to 4 minutes. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time.
Stir in the cooled melted chocolate. Sift in {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, and {{baking-powder}}. Fold in alternating with {{soured-cream}} until just combined.
Divide between the two tins. Bake for 32 to 35 minutes until a skewer comes out with moist crumbs. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn onto racks and cool completely.

Beat the second quantity of {{unsalted-butter}} until very pale, about 2 minutes. Sift in {{icing-sugar}} and {{cocoa-powder}} in three additions, beating between each. Add {{whole-milk}} and beat for 3 minutes until smooth and light.
Place one sponge on a board or stand. Spread with half the buttercream. Place the second sponge on top. Use the remaining buttercream to coat the top and sides thinly.
Refrigerate the assembled cake for 30 minutes while you make the ganache.
Heat {{double-cream}} to a simmer and pour over the remaining 200g of {{dark-chocolate}}. Stir until completely smooth. Leave to cool for 15 to 20 minutes until thick but still pourable.
Pour the ganache over the chilled cake from the centre, letting it spread and drip down the sides. Leave at room temperature for at least 20 minutes before serving.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: This is a reliable cake for all its richness.

items.symptom: Sponge layers cracking or crumbling when stacked.
items.cause: Not fully cooled, or turned out too soon.
items.fix: Cool completely on the rack before frosting. A warm sponge will melt the buttercream and slide.

items.symptom: Ganache set too firm before pouring.
items.fix: Return the ganache bowl to the warm water bath for 30 seconds and stir. It should flow from a spoon in a slow ribbon.
